text: I am sharing a case of a robotic sigmoid resection for a small cancer in a polyp that was incompletely resected. There was residual disease at the site on a repeat endoscopic view. The patient was offered attempted endoscopic resection versus formal surgical oncologic resection, and they chose oncologic resection.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to burn the area marked by a tattoo, preparing the site for further inspection with flexible endoscopy later in the procedure.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, tracing along the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to achieve high ligation, aided by the patient's favorable body habitus with minimal visceral fat.

The surgeon visually identifies the left ureter during lateral dissection, noting the absence of stents. The ureter becomes visible as the dissection progresses laterally. The dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) continues towards its root at the aorta.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter before proceeding with the high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The vascular sheath and adventitia of the IMA are carefully cleaned, revealing a clear plane for sealing the vessel using an energy device or clips.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection while taking the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The redundant sigmoid colon and mesentery are visible, indicating that high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) or mobilization of the splenic flexure is not necessary.

The surgeon joins the medial dissection, with a visible sponge placed to protect the ureter from inadvertent injury. The lateral dissection is performed, although the full extent is not shown, and the splenic flexure is not mobilized.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectosigmoid area, ensuring the ureter remains lateral to the dissection site to avoid injury. The rectus sigmoid junction is cleaned using a vessel sealer, with the rectum already mobilized to facilitate the upcoming anastomosis.

The surgeon approaches the bowel wall from underneath the transected inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), with the rectum mobilized for anastomosis. The redundant sigmoid colon and mesentery are visible, and the lymph node yield is noted.

The surgeon uses a flexible endoscope to visualize the tumor, aligning it with the location of the vessel sealer. The endoscope confirms a six-centimeter proximal margin from the tumor, and perfusion is checked.

I consider doing a transrectal extraction of the specimen in this case. In the end I elected against it. I didn't want any chance that I was going to tear the rectum pulling this specimen out and I don't morsel the specimen in cancer cases.

The surgeon divides the bowel at proximal and distal points, ensuring a ninety-degree angle across the anti-mesenteric surface. A stay suture is placed in the far corner to align the staple line for the hands-on anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 9-inch V-lock suture to join the two staple lines, forming the posterior outer row of a two-layer anastomosis. The suture aligns the tissue neatly and is applied efficiently.

You know, I like to do intracorporeal anastomosis and extraction of the specimen at the end of the case. I think it saves time, particularly robotics, so you don't have to undock in the middle and then either redock or finish the case laparoscopically. I just have moved away from EEA staplers.

The surgeon completes the outer back row of the anastomosis and uses scissors to perform a proctotomy followed by an enterotomy. The incision is made approximately one centimeter away from the end, ensuring a wide open anastomosis without ischemic concerns.

The surgeon begins sewing the inner posterior row of the anastomosis, starting at the posterior midline and stitching away from themselves. Each stitch is cinched after placement to ensure precise alignment.
The surgeon takes a bite on the rectal side and parks the suture down in the pelvis, preparing to transition to a canal over the top. A new stitch is started in the middle of the surgical field.

The surgeon performs the inner posterior row of sutures, sewing towards themselves and using a baseball stitch technique to secure the lip created at the enterotomy, approximately one centimeter away from the staple line. Each suture is carefully guided into place as the stitching progresses around the corner.

The surgeon transitions back to the outer layer, using the inner stitch to sew away from themselves, coming around the near corner. This technique inverts the outside lip effectively. The surgeon continues suturing with another suture, navigating challenging angles to complete the in-out stitching method.
The surgeon achieves a complete inversion of the anterior row of the anastomosis, tying the two ends together. The posterior row is then run over the top, joining the two staple lines.
The surgeon ties the suture to the silk stay suture to complete the anastomosis. A flexible endoscope is inserted through the anastomosis to perform a leak test, checking for patency and bleeding.

No trouble coming up and through that anastomosis, widely patent. This patient did very well, was discharged on post-operative day one. Again, pathology T one and zero was doing very well at her two week follow up. Specimen was subsequently at the end of the case extracted through a fin and steel incision and was discharged with five pills of oxycodone, which they said they didn't take. Thank you for watching.
